narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was carefully evaluated by our admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam outcomes based on academic and personal profiles like yours. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 sits below the typical 3.21 average, while your LSAT score of 32.50 is under the usual 36.70, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles of 2 and 3 respectively fall short of the dataset averages around 5-6. You're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of students in our data. The model estimates a 55% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the scales just enough to lead to this rejection—it's a close call, but universities use this to prioritize applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. The biggest factor pulling against you was your Year 3 decile ranking, which signaled weaker performance relative to peers and strongly nudged the prediction toward failure. Your family income bracket had a similar pushing effect toward that riskier outcome. Meanwhile, your undergraduate GPA was the top factor working in your favor, countering the risk by showing some foundational strength. Your LSAT score, Year 1 decile, and full-time status also leaned slightly toward predicting success, but not enough to outweigh the concerns. Compared to typical students who get approved, your metrics are a notch below in these key areas, especially the later-year grades that often predict bar readiness best. This isn't the end of your path to law—consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, strengthening your upper-level deciles through targeted coursework, or exploring part-time programs if that fits your life better. Many applicants in similar spots improve these markers and reapply successfully; we're rooting for your next steps.
